flashcards.front: What is an Aggregation View in DBMS?
flashcards.back: An Aggregation View is a database view that contains pre-computed summarized data (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) from one or more base tables. It acts as a virtual or materialized table that stores query results to improve performance for analytical queries.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between Materialized and Virtual (Logical) Aggregation Views.
flashcards.back: Materialized views physically store aggregated data on disk and must be periodically refreshed to sync with base tables. Virtual views store only the query definition and compute results at runtime—no storage overhead but slower queries. Oracle supports both; materialized views use more space but offer faster reads.

flashcards.front: Write SQL to create a Materialized Aggregation View with FAST refresh.
flashcards.back: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW sales_monthly_mv BUILD IMMEDIATE REFRESH FAST START WITH SYSDATE NEXT SYSDATE + INTERVAL '1' MONTH AS SELECT s.store_id, TRUNC(s.sale_date, 'MM') AS month, SUM(s.amount) AS total_sales, COUNT(*) AS num_transactions FROM sales s GROUP BY s.store_id, TRUNC(s.sale_date, 'MM');

flashcards.front: Explain the different refresh mechanisms for Materialized Aggregation Views.
flashcards.back: Four refresh types: (1) COMPLETE—rebuilds entire view from scratch; (2) FAST—incremental refresh using materialized view logs; (3) FORCE—tries FAST, falls back to COMPLETE if not possible; (4) NEVER—disables refresh. Modes: ON COMMIT (after each DML), ON DEMAND (manual), or scheduled with NEXT clause.

flashcards.front: What is Query Rewrite in the context of Aggregation Views?
flashcards.back: Query Rewrite is an Oracle optimizer feature that automatically detects when a user query can be answered using an existing materialized view instead of accessing base tables. It transparently redirects SELECT statements to use pre-computed aggregates, significantly improving performance without application changes.

flashcards.front: List three advantages and two disadvantages of using Materialized Aggregation Views.
flashcards.back: Advantages: (1) Dramatically faster query execution for reporting/OLAP; (2) Reduced I/O and network traffic; (3) Simplified application code. Disadvantages: (1) Storage overhead—data duplicated; (2) Data staleness until next refresh; (3) Additional maintenance overhead for refresh scheduling.

flashcards.front: How does an Aggregation View differ from a Regular View in DBMS?
flashcards.back: Regular (non-materialized) views are virtual—they store only the SELECT query, not data. Each access executes the underlying query. Aggregation views can be materialized (storing computed results) or virtual. Materialized aggregation views consume storage but provide constant-time access; regular views always compute fresh results but may be slow for complex aggregations.

flashcards.front: State two practical use cases where Aggregation Views are essential in enterprise databases.
flashcards.back: Use Case 1: Data warehouse reporting—monthly/quarterly sales summaries pre-calculated for dashboards. Use Case 2: OLAP systems—aggregating millions of transaction records into regional/product-wise totals for trend analysis. Both scenarios require repeated aggregation of large datasets where real-time computation is impractical.
